review.summary: The series is close on the basic direction, but the visible patches still have correctness gaps in how endianness is selected. One boot path still hard-codes little-endian ELF loading, and the new runtime helper is being reused in places where the spec requires page-table or effective-access endianness rather than current privilege endianness.

stages.A: Reviewed the cover letter and the visible diffs for patches 1-5. The series adds a per-CPU big-endian property, propagates runtime data endianness into translation/helpers, updates boot ROM/data generation, and adjusts PTW handling.
stages.B: The implementation has one obvious functional regression in the boot loader path and two architectural issues in the endianness selection logic.
stages.C: Risk is high because the affected paths are early boot, page-table walking, and translated load/store generation. Failures here present as boot hangs or hard-to-diagnose memory corruption.
stages.D: The added functional coverage appears too narrow to catch these cases. In particular, it does not appear to exercise BE ELF loading, SBE!=UBE, or MPRV/virtualization-driven effective-endianness changes.
stages.E: Recommendation: fix the endian source for each access class before merging, then add focused tests for BE ELF boot and for privilege combinations where effective endianness differs from env->priv.

findings.title: BE kernel ELF loading is still hard-wired to little-endian
findings.description: The commit message says ELF loading should switch between ELFDATA2MSB and ELFDATA2LSB based on hart endianness, but the actual call still passes ELFDATA2LSB unconditionally. A big-endian RISC-V ELF kernel will therefore be parsed as little-endian, which is likely to reject the image or load it incorrectly.
patch_context: -    kernel_size = load_elf_ram_sym(kernel_filename, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL,
+    kernel_size = load_elf_ram_sym(kernel_filename, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL,
findings.suggestion: Select the ELF data encoding from the hart/boot info, e.g. `info->is_big_endian ? ELFDATA2MSB : ELFDATA2LSB`, and add a functional test that boots a BE ELF kernel rather than only a raw image path.
findings.confidence_reason: The diff shows the constant remains `ELFDATA2LSB` despite the changelog claiming runtime selection.

findings.title: Page-table walks use current-privilege endianness instead of page-table endianness
findings.description: The page-table walker now keys off `riscv_cpu_data_is_big_endian(env)`, but that helper selects UBE/SBE/MBE from `env->priv`. For a U-mode access, first-stage page-table structures are still supervisor-managed and must not use UBE. If UBE differs from SBE, the walker will read PTEs with the wrong byte order and fault incorrectly.
findings.suggestion: Do not derive PTW endianness from `env->priv`. Compute PTW endianness from the translation stage being walked: SBE/MBE for supervisor-level structures, and VSBE for VS-stage walks where applicable.
findings.confidence_reason: The visible code clearly uses the generic helper in the walker, and that helper is documented in patch 3 as switching on current privilege rather than walk stage.

findings.title: Runtime data-endianness helper ignores effective access mode such as MPRV and virtualization
findings.description: `riscv_cpu_data_is_big_endian()` keys only on `env->priv`. That is not sufficient for all explicit data accesses: M-mode loads/stores under MPRV use the effective privilege specified by MPP, and virtualized accesses can be controlled by virtualization-specific endianness state. Because the translator also snapshots this helper into TB state, translated accesses can execute with the wrong byte order whenever the effective access mode differs from the current privilege mode.
patch_context: + * Return true if data accesses are big-endian for the current privilege
+ * level, based on the MSTATUS MBE/SBE/UBE bits.
findings.suggestion: Split 'current privilege endianness' from 'effective memory access endianness'. The latter should account for MPRV/MPP and virtualization state, and any state that affects translated access endianness must be included in TB keying.
findings.confidence_reason: This depends on QEMU's exact effective-access model, but the helper as written only covers `env->priv` and is reused broadly for translated data accesses.

cover_letter:
Subject: [PATCH v5 0/7] Add RISC-V big-endian target support
From: Djordje Todorovic <Djordje.Todorovic@htecgroup.com>
Date: Tue, 24 Mar 2026 16:40:14 +0000
Message-Id: <20260324164007.549397-1-djordje.todorovic@htecgroup.com>
MIME-Version: 1.0
Content-Type: text/plain; charset="utf-8"
Content-Transfer-Encoding: 7bit

Adding functional test case for riscv big-endian.

Djordje Todorovic (7):
  target/riscv: Add big-endian CPU property
  target/riscv: Set endianness MSTATUS bits at CPU reset
  target/riscv: Implement runtime data endianness via MSTATUS bits
  hw/riscv: Make boot code endianness-aware at runtime
  target/riscv: Fix page table walk endianness for big-endian harts
  target/riscv: Support runtime endianness in virtio via sysemu callback
  target/riscv: Add test for RISC-V BE
